findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change 5062's backfill label from `source=backfill_structural` to `source=backfill_classified` to match the parent's single-backfill-vocabulary constraint and c085's source enum.
evidence: 5062 Approach: 'Backfilled classes carry source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified'
Parent SC5: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled source=backfill_classified and confidence=classified (the single backfill vocabulary — there is no backfill_structural)'
c085 AC3: 'source in {structural,git,sidecar,snapshot,backfill_classified}' — no backfill_structural
findings.finding: Child 5062 persists backfill labels as `source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified`, but the parent explicitly states 'the single backfill vocabulary — there is no `backfill_structural`', and c085 enumerates the allowed source values as {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}. `backfill_structural` is absent from that set, so 5062's output would be unrecognized by the registry and `is_authoritative()` logic.
findings.location: child 5062-30a8-9153-4c61 — Approach section ('source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified') and AC2
scenarios: 5062 backfill runs and writes records with source=backfill_structural; c085's is_authoritative() doesn't recognise it and may default to treating it as authoritative (or raise a key error), corrupting the authoritative series segregation guarantee
AC5 requires at least one backfilled value with source=backfill_classified; 5062 writes none, leaving AC5 partially unsatisfied by this child's output unless another child covers it
findings.suggested_fix: In scripts/backfill_bug_class.py and test_backfill_bug_class.py replace every occurrence of source='backfill_structural' with source='backfill_classified'. Update 5062 AC2 to assert source='backfill_classified' specifically, not just 'source is present'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether c595 backfill snapshots should use `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified` (per parent SC5) or define a distinct sub-label; update c085's is_authoritative() and c595's ACs accordingly so backfill complexity data is never aggregated into the authoritative series.
evidence: c595 Approach: 'writes through the same snapshot.py writer … snapshots are labeled source=snapshot, confidence=high (deterministic tool output)'
Parent SC5: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled source=backfill_classified and confidence=classified'
c085 approach: 'source in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}' — snapshot is treated as authoritative by is_authoritative()
findings.finding: Child c595 writes backfill snapshots labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`, which `is_authoritative()` in c085 would classify as authoritative — yet these are one-time historical backfill values checked out from old commits. This contradicts the parent success criterion SC5 ('every backfilled/classified value is labeled source=backfill_classified and confidence=classified') and the parent AC3 segregation guarantee.
findings.location: child c595-9348-d4f2-4529 — Approach section ('source=snapshot, confidence=high') and AC1
scenarios: A user queries the complexity trend; backfill-derived historical points labeled source=snapshot pass is_authoritative() as True and are aggregated into the authoritative series, even though they were reconstructed by checking out old commits — defeating the authoritative-vs-backfill segregation
The parent AC3 unit test (is_authoritative('backfill_classified') is False) passes, but c595 backfill enters the authoritative series undetected through the snapshot label
findings.suggested_fix: Label c595 backfill snapshots source='backfill_classified', confidence='classified' (consistent with parent SC5). If deterministic tool-output backfill warrants a higher confidence, define a distinct source label (e.g. backfill_deterministic) in c085's vocabulary and update is_authoritative() to return False for it, then update c595's AC1 to assert that label.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix 1f77 AC3 and the test.yml step to target `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (the git-tracked path), not `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (the gitignored path).
evidence: 1f77 AC3: 'the test.yml change appends coverage to reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson'

Parent Approach (Snapshot store path): 'committed to .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson — a git-tracked path in the rebar store — NOT under reports/, which is gitignored (.gitignore:74) and would silently drop every snapshot'
3275 Approach: 'It lives under the tracked .rebar/ store, NOT under reports/'
findings.finding: Child 1f77 AC3 specifies that the `test.yml` change 'appends coverage to `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`', which is the gitignored path. The parent explicitly states the snapshot store is `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (a git-tracked path) and that `/reports/` is gitignored (`.gitignore:74`) so snapshots there are silently dropped. Child 3275 correctly targets `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`.
findings.location: child 1f77-f8c0-bfae-4a79 — AC3 ('appends coverage to reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson')
scenarios: The test.yml step runs successfully (exits 0) but writes to reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson, which is gitignored; the file is never committed; the GHA coverage trend silently never accumulates; the going-forward series 3275 builds stays empty for coverage
findings.suggested_fix: In scripts/harvest_gha.py and the test.yml step, replace the snapshot output path reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson with .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson. Update 1f77 AC3 to assert the added step targets .rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson.

findings.finding: Child 5062 labels backfilled close_class records `source=backfill_structural` but the parent plan explicitly states there is no `backfill_structural` and defines a single backfill vocabulary of `source=backfill_classified`. This contradicts the parent's SC5 and AC3/AC5, and means 5062's output may be treated as authoritative by c085's `is_authoritative()` helper (which only excludes `backfill_classified`), polluting the authoritative series.
findings.location: child 5062-30a8-9153-4c61, Approach section: 'Backfilled classes carry `source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified`'
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` and `tests/unit/scripts/test_backfill_bug_class.py`, replace every occurrence of `source=backfill_structural` with `source=backfill_classified` to match the parent-mandated single vocabulary.

findings.finding: Child c595 labels backfill complexity/clone snapshots `source=snapshot, confidence=high`, but these are historical backfilled values and the parent requires ALL backfilled values carry `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified`. Using `source=snapshot` causes them to be treated as authoritative by c085's `is_authoritative()`, violating the parent's segregation guarantee for backfill data.
findings.location: child c595-9348-d4f2-4529, Approach section: 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`'
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`, write snapshots with `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified` (matching the parent's single backfill vocabulary). Update c595's AC to assert those labels, and update c085's is_authoritative() to exclude backfill_classified consistently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to ed13 (or a dedicated test) that captures the pre-epic `rebar transition --help` output as a golden baseline and asserts the post-epic help shows no net increase in the count of required bug-close arguments.
evidence: Parent AC4: 'a rebar transition --help diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments' and 'verified by diffing rebar transition --help against the pre-epic baseline'
ed13 ACs: tests exit codes and jq output for close_class, but no AC mentions capturing/diffing against a pre-epic --help baseline snapshot
findings.finding: The parent AC4 requires verifying the bug-close `--help` diff against a stored pre-epic baseline ('verified by diffing `rebar transition --help` against the pre-epic baseline'). No child AC owns this explicit snapshot/diff-against-baseline check; ed13's ACs test the behavioral outcome (close exits nonzero without --class) but do not capture or compare a stored pre-epic help snapshot.
findings.location: child ed13-e224-1ece-44ff — AC set; parent AC4
scenarios: ed13 lands, tests pass, but a subtle extra required argument is introduced in transition.py that does not affect the close path tests; the missing baseline-diff check fails to catch it; the parent AC4 completion verifier fails at epic close
findings.suggested_fix: In tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_bug_close_class.py add a test that runs `rebar transition --help`, parses the required-argument list for the bug-close path, and asserts the count equals 1 (--class replacing --reason). Optionally check a stored golden baseline file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify git-blame feasibility: add a spike/prototype demonstrating the exact subprocess call shape (`git blame --porcelain <fix>~1 -- <path>`), the working-directory resolution relative to rebar's source-repo vs. tickets-branch layout, and the porcelain output parsing — before committing the auto-derivation architecture.
evidence: Plan: 'caused_by auto-derivation blames the pre-fix state — the parent of the fixing commit (<fix>~1) — over the bug's recorded file_impact'
Codebase search for 'git blame' in src/rebar returns zero hits
src/rebar/attest/authorship.py uses 'git log --diff-filter=A --full-history' exclusively, never git-blame
src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py: run_git() exists but is never invoked with blame arguments anywhere in the codebase
Plan states rebar is 'harness- and target-project-agnostic' — source-repo CWD for blame is never pinned
findings.finding: The plan asserts git-blame-based `caused_by` auto-derivation (`git blame <fix>~1`) as a capability, but the codebase has zero git-blame invocations anywhere (authorship.py, attest/, gitutil.py all use git-log, never git-blame). The source-repo working directory for the blame call is unspecified — rebar is harness-agnostic and the tickets branch is a separate orphan tree from the source repo. No prior-art search or proof-of-concept validates that the subprocess shape, porcelain output parsing, and working-directory resolution work for the agent-harness deployments rebar targets.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Blame-derivation is best-effort and non-authoritative' + 'Auto-link vs the closed-source guard (555e ordering)'
scenarios: Blame runs in the wrong working directory (tickets branch CWD vs. source repo CWD), yielding a 'fatal: no such path' and silently skipping all auto-derivation
Porcelain output format differs across git versions present in agent environments, causing parse failures
Agent environments where the source repo is a separate remote mount (opcert workspace pattern) have no local history to blame against

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State explicitly what the event store looks like after rollback for both `caused_by` LINK events and `close_class` STATUS events, assert that a rolled-back rebar binary tolerates them (e.g. cite the `additionalProperties: true` tolerance on `ticket_state.schema.json` and confirm the `relation` enum validator is not applied on reduce), and name a compensating-forward cleanup script if the new values must be purged.
evidence: Epic plan Approach: 'back-out is simply ceasing to require/write the field'
Ticket 0b96 Approach: 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'

ABSENCE: neither the plan nor any child ticket asserts what the store looks like after rollback — do existing `caused_by` LINK events or `close_class` STATUS events cause problems for an older rebar binary that does not recognise the `caused_by` relation?
common.schema.json `relation` enum is strict (no additionalProperties safety valve at the relation level) — a binary rolled back before 0b96 would see `caused_by` as an unknown enum value.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback description for `caused_by` links ('back-out is removing the additive links') and for `close_class` ('back-out = stop requiring/writing the field') does not assert the schema state AFTER rollback — specifically, already-written LINK events with `caused_by` and STATUS events carrying `close_class` remain permanently in the event store, and no tooling or procedure is named to remove them.
findings.location: Approach § 'back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change' and ticket 0b96 Approach § 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'
scenarios: After landing 0b96 and writing several `caused_by` LINK events, a rollback removes `caused_by` from the `Relation` Literal in types.py; existing LINK events in the store still carry `caused_by`, and any strict enum validation in the rolled-back binary rejects or logs warnings for them on every reduce.

After rolling back `close_class`, existing STATUS events carry a `close_class` key not recognised by the rolled-back reducer — if `process_status` ever grows a strict-key check, this silently corrupts those tickets.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to 0b96 and ed13 confirming that reduce-time validation is permissive for unknown enum values (cite the specific code path), and add a `scripts/purge_caused_by_links.py --dry-run` script reference as the compensating-forward path so rollback instructions are complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in blame.py's design the exact `git blame` flags (e.g., `--porcelain -l`) and the algorithm for computing 'dominant culprit' from per-line output (e.g., majority-commit-by-line-count threshold), and document the subprocess error path for deleted/renamed files at fix~1.
evidence: Plan: 'Blames the pre-fix state — the parent of the fixing commit (<fix>~1) — over the bug's recorded file_impact'
Plan: 'Resolves the dominant culprit commit and maps it to a ticket via its rebar-ticket: trailer. Returns None on ambiguity: no file_impact, multi-commit spread, rename/--follow uncertainty'
ABSENCE: no git blame usage exists anywhere in src/rebar/ (confirmed by search)
ABSENCE: plan does not specify git blame output format flags (--porcelain vs default) or the aggregation algorithm that determines 'dominant'
Unverified: behavior when file_impact path doesn't exist at fix~1 (deleted file blame fails unless --follow or git blame handles gracefully)
findings.finding: The git blame integration for caused_by auto-derivation (blame.py, ticket 555e) introduces a new external git CLI surface with unverified behavioral specifics: the plan does not specify which git blame flags are used (e.g., --porcelain, -l, -C for copy detection), how 'dominant culprit commit' is operationalized from the per-line blame output (the plan says 'single dominant culprit' but gives no algorithm), or what happens when a file_impact path was deleted/renamed between fix~1 and the blame invocation (requires --follow or explicit error handling). Git blame has no prior use in this codebase (search confirmed zero hits), so there is no precedent to inherit from.
findings.location: Approach section ('Blame-derivation is best-effort and non-authoritative'), ticket 555e (blame.py description), ticket 2f8e (backfill_caused_by.py)
scenarios: A bug's file_impact path was deleted in the fixing commit itself — git blame <fix>~1 -- <path> may error or return no output, and the plan's 'skip on failure' behavior depends on whether blame.py handles this subprocess failure path explicitly.
A file with many committers produces ambiguous blame output — 'dominant culprit' algorithm is undefined, risking either silent wrong-edge emission or implementation divergence from the spec.

Default git blame output (non-porcelain) is notoriously hard to parse reliably; if the implementer uses porcelain format, a unit test with a real temp git repo is required but the test fixture approach is only partially specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ticket 555e's Approach: 'blame.py uses `git blame --porcelain <fix>~1 -- <path>` per file_impact entry; dominant culprit = the commit SHA appearing in >50% of blamed lines (else skip); a non-zero git blame exit or empty output skips the file silently.'

evidence: docs/ticket-model.md: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_commands/transition.py: 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169 & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py: 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169 & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
tests/golden/cli_help/__overview_help.out: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what `accruing_since` represents (e.g. 'the ISO-8601 date of the earliest event in the store that could contribute to this metric, or null if none exists') so the unavailable-state logic in `9a5a` is unambiguous.
evidence: 'every registered metric key is present with either a value or {"unavailable":{"reason","accruing_since"}}' — `accruing_since` is named but not defined
'metrics whose signals have not yet accrued report a structured `unavailable` state and light up automatically as data arrives' — describes behavior but not what `accruing_since` contains
findings.finding: The `accruing_since` field in the `unavailable` structured response is referenced in the AC (`{"unavailable":{"reason","accruing_since"}}`) but the plan never defines what value populates `accruing_since` — it could mean the date the metric was first registered, the date the earliest relevant event exists in the store, or the date the first data point within the requested range was seen. An agent implementing the unavailable-state logic (ticket `9a5a`) cannot write a deterministic value without this definition.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2: `{"unavailable":{"reason","accruing_since"}}`
scenarios: Agent writes a registry entry for a metric with no data yet; must choose what timestamp to emit for `accruing_since` — metric registration date? first event date? `null`?
The `jq -e` integration test asserting no missing keys will pass regardless of what value is emitted, so a wrong value silently passes CI
findings.suggested_fix: State the default: `accruing_since` is the timestamp of the earliest raw event in the store that would feed this metric (derived structurally at query time), or `null` if no such event exists. This is derivable without a separate write path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the pre-epic `rebar transition --help` output is committed as a golden fixture (e.g. `tests/golden/cli_help/transition_help_pre_epic.out`) before any `ed13` changes land, so the diff check in AC4 is mechanically reproducible in-session.
evidence: 'verified by diffing `rebar transition --help` against the pre-epic baseline (the change swaps one required arg for another, adds none)'
No plan section specifies where the pre-epic baseline is stored or how it is captured before work begins
findings.finding: The 'pre-epic baseline' required for the `rebar transition --help` diff check (AC4) is not specified as a captured artifact. The AC says 'verified by diffing `rebar transition --help` against the pre-epic baseline' but does not state where this baseline is recorded (a committed golden file, a test fixture, a PR description snapshot). Without a captured baseline, the in-session diff check cannot be performed mechanically.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: 'verified by diffing `rebar transition --help` against the pre-epic baseline'
scenarios: Agent completes `ed13`, tries to run the diff check, has no baseline file to diff against
A future reader cannot verify the no-increase invariant without knowing where the baseline was captured
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'Before any `ed13` changes, capture `rebar transition --help > tests/golden/cli_help/transition_help_pre_epic.out` and commit it; the AC4 check diffs the post-`ed13` output against this file.'

findings.finding: The `unavailable` state's `accruing_since` field has no specified population rule. The plan and child ticket c085 say the field must be 'an ISO `accruing_since`' (c085 AC2) and 'light up automatically as data arrives' (epic Success Criteria), but never specifies what timestamp value to write: the epic creation date, the first event in the store, the current wall-clock time, or some metric-specific anchor. This is a processing protocol referenced but not co-located with the schema.
findings.location: Success Criteria ('metrics whose signals have not yet accrued report a structured `unavailable` state'); child ticket c085 AC2 ('a non-empty `reason` and an ISO `accruing_since`')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the registry contract: '`accruing_since` is the ISO date of the tracker's earliest event (from `.env-id` creation timestamp), giving each metric a consistent 'data starts here' anchor that is the same for all structural readers and 0001-01-01 for a brand-new store.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which tool(s) will compute complexity and clone metrics for the going-forward snapshot series, whether they become declared rebar dependencies (e.g., added to `pyproject.toml` extras), and how `rebar metrics` degrades to `{"unavailable": ...}` when the tool is absent — resolving the capability assertion before committing the metric-registry design.
evidence: src/rebar/grounding/engine_b.py:59: 'Metric tools (size/complexity). Neither ships with rebar; absent -> abstain(no_tool)'
src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py:337: 'scc/lizard are NOT installed here, so this fails open'
docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md:22-26: radon, jscpd are 'pip install' / 'npx' manual installs, not package deps
Plan mentions 'complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits' as part of Backfill track, and code-health as a first-class lens in the metrics command
Plan never names the specific tool for going-forward complexity collection or declares it as a new dependency
findings.finding: The plan claims complexity and clone metrics will be captured in the going-forward snapshot series (metric registry, child 3275), but the only tools that compute these (radon/lizard for complexity, jscpd for clones) are NOT installed as rebar package dependencies — they are optional manual tools documented only in `docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md`. The existing `engine_b_backends._run_metric` already documents this: 'scc/lizard are NOT installed here, so this fails open'. The plan never specifies what tool computes complexity/clone for the going-forward series, whether it will be added as a dependency, or how the metric gracefully degrades to `unavailable` when the tool is absent in all CI environments.
findings.location: Context section: 'code-health movement (coverage/complexity/clone) is computed ephemerally in CI and never persisted' / Decomposition: 'Backfill: complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits'
scenarios: The metric registry registers complexity/clone keys but every CI run and every `rebar metrics` invocation reports them as `unavailable` because no tool is installed — the metric never lights up
The backfill child (complexity/clone snapshots) cannot be executed without a non-trivial manual tool install that is not part of rebar's install surface

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the migration AC to explicitly verify removal: assert that `rebar transition --help` shows `--reason` absent (or demoted to optional-and-deprecated) AND `--class` present as required, not just that the net count is unchanged.
evidence: AC: 'the required free-text reason is replaced (not supplemented) by a bounded --class enum... a rebar transition --help diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments'
Success Criteria (operationalized): 'the change swaps one required arg for another, adds none'
Gap: 'no net increase' is satisfied by making --reason optional (not removed) and adding required --class; the AC verify step would pass even though --reason still exists.
findings.finding: The bug-close migration AC verifies only that the net required-argument count does not increase ('no net increase'), but does not explicitly verify that `--reason` is removed — a swap could be obscured by making `--reason` optional while adding required `--class`.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 4: 'a rebar transition --help diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments'
scenarios: Implementer makes --reason optional instead of removing it; help diff shows net +0 required args; AC passes but the migration is incomplete and the free-text field is still writable, polluting the event store.
Test harness diffs only the count of required args, not their identity, masking incomplete removal.
findings.suggested_fix: Amend AC 4 verify step to: '`rebar transition --help` diff shows `--reason` removed from required args AND `--class` added as required, confirmed by grepping the help output for the absence of `--reason` in the required-arguments section.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `close_class` enum values in a single authoritative location (e.g., a constants module or schema file) that the CLI parser, event writer, and metrics reader all import from, so adding a new class value is a one-line change.
evidence: "a REQUIRED bounded `--class` enum"
"its error message must enumerate the allowed values"
No mention of a central enum definition, a registry, or a single source of truth for the allowed class values
findings.finding: The `--class` enum is described as a 'bounded' required argument on bug close, but the plan does not state where the enum values are centrally defined or how they can be extended without touching multiple files (CLI parser, STATUS event writer, test fixtures, metrics reader). If the allowed classification set evolves — a routine product decision — there is no indication that a single-point-of-change exists, making the set effectively hardcoded across the implementation.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'bug classification via a persisted close `--class` enum replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text reason'; Approach: 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13)'
scenarios: A new bug classification category (e.g., 'security') needs to be added six months post-launch; a developer must hunt down and update the CLI choices list, the STATUS event schema, the metrics reader's class-distribution aggregation, and golden test fixtures separately, with no compile-time or test-time enforcement that all sites are in sync.
findings.suggested_fix: Create `src/rebar/constants/bug_close_class.py` (or equivalent) exporting a `BugCloseClass` enum; have the CLI `choices=`, the STATUS event writer, and the metrics reader all reference it. The error message can be generated from `[e.value for e in BugCloseClass]`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the 555e plan (or the epic Approach section) whether the auto-link runs before or after `_compact_on_close` in `close_ticket`, and confirm the AC's `jq -e '.links[]'` probe against `rebar show` (compiled state) works correctly either way.
evidence: Plan: 'The auto-link runs *after* `txn.transition_core` commits the STATUS event, so the bug (the link SOURCE) is already `closed` when blame runs'
`close_ticket` in `transition_close.py` sequence (verified): txn.transition_core → signing → _compact_on_close(threshold=0) → push
_compact_on_close docstring: '--threshold=0 --skip-sync, commit kept' — folds ALL events
_write_link_event docstring: 'no cycle check, no idempotency' — the new LINK.json would be folded if it exists when compact runs
findings.finding: The plan places the `caused_by` auto-link call 'after `txn.transition_core` commits the STATUS event' (Approach section, 555e ticket) but does not specify whether it runs before or after `_compact_on_close` in `close_ticket`. `_compact_on_close` is called with `--threshold=0`, meaning it immediately folds ALL events including any LINK.json just written. This ordering affects the observable state: if the auto-link runs before `_compact_on_close`, the LINK is baked into the compiled_state SNAPSHOT and no raw LINK.json exists; if after, a raw LINK.json exists. Ticket 555e's AC verifies the link via `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[]'` — which reads from compiled/reduced state and works either way — but the test also needs to correctly probe `_is_active_link` behavior (which checks both LINK.json files and SNAPSHOT deps[]). The plan (and child 555e) leave the exact insertion point in `close_ticket` unspecified, creating implementation ambiguity that could produce a subtly different observable shape than the ACs probe.
findings.location: Approach section ('Auto-link vs the closed-source guard (555e ordering)') and ticket 555e file_impact: `transition_close.py`
scenarios: Auto-link written before _compact_on_close: LINK.json is immediately folded into SNAPSHOT compiled_state.deps[]; no raw LINK.json is present for `_is_active_link`'s first-pass scan, but the SNAPSHOT fallback path catches it. Observable shape differs from the non-compacted case.
Auto-link written after _compact_on_close: LINK.json persists as a raw event file. Two commits happen (STATUS commit then LINK commit after push). This is the cleaner observable shape for the ACs.
findings.suggested_fix: Insert the auto-link call AFTER `_compact_on_close` (and before the push) so it is never immediately re-folded into the SNAPSHOT, the raw LINK.json is observable, and the two-commit sequence (STATUS, then LINK) is clearly separated. Update 555e's file_impact note on `transition_close.py` to say 'after _compact_on_close'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `--batch-size N` (or equivalent progress-checkpoint) option to `backfill_caused_by.py` so a large closed-bug population can be processed in bounded chunks and a killed run restarts from where it left off rather than re-blaming all bugs from scratch.
evidence: Ticket 2f8e approach: 'iterate closed bugs, reuse src/rebar/metrics/blame.py, draw caused_by on high-confidence single culprits'
No mention of chunk size, rate limit, or progress file in 2f8e description or ACs.
ABSENCE: neither the epic plan nor ticket 2f8e specifies a --batch-size or checkpoint mechanism.
findings.finding: The `backfill_caused_by.py` script (ticket 2f8e) iterates all closed bugs and runs `git blame` per bug with no batching or throttling specification; on a repo with hundreds of closed bugs this is a single unbatched, potentially slow operation with no progress checkpointing.
findings.location: Decomposition § Backfill track / ticket 2f8e (`scripts/backfill_caused_by.py`)
scenarios: A project with 500 closed bugs triggers 500 sequential `git blame` subprocess calls in a single run; the script is killed halfway through CI timeout, leaving no record of which bugs were already processed (the skip-if-linked check rescues this only if the LINK write committed before the kill).
A network-attached git remote causes each blame call to be slow; the script exhausts a CI job timeout.
findings.suggested_fix: Process closed bugs in chunks of N (configurable, default 50); write a `.rebar/backfill-caused-by.progress` marker after each successful chunk commit so a re-run skips already-processed bugs without re-running blame.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit caller-side rollback/expand-contract step to the ed13 plan: either (a) a transition period accepting both `--reason` (deprecated, warned) and `--class`, with a cutover date, or (b) a pre-landing audit step that identifies and migrates all `--reason` call-sites, documented in the ticket, so the back-out path for callers is unambiguous.
evidence: 'back-out is simply ceasing to require/write the field' — addresses data writer only, not CLI callers
'the required-argument count for a bug close does not increase from 1 to 2' — acknowledges the swap but does not describe dual-accept
No mention of a deprecation window where both `--reason` and `--class` are accepted simultaneously
No explicit caller-migration plan or period (grep/notify existing callers of `--reason`)
findings.finding: The bug-close CLI swap (`--reason` → `--class`) is a hard cut with no expand-contract intermediate phase, meaning any existing automated caller (CI script, alias) that passes `--reason` breaks immediately on ed13 landing, and the described rollback ('ceasing to require/write the field') only addresses the writer/data side — it does not specify how callers that have already migrated to `--class` revert, nor does it mention a dual-accept period.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13)'
scenarios: A CI pipeline that calls `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason 'fixed'` breaks the moment ed13 lands, with no warning period
If ed13 must be reverted after some callers have migrated to `--class`, the rollback path is not specified for those callers
findings.suggested_fix: In the ed13 section, add: 'Pre-landing: grep the repo and CI configs for `--reason` on bug-close invocations and enumerate them; either migrate atomically or add a one-release dual-accept shim (`--reason` accepted but prints a deprecation warning, `--class` derived from reason if mappable). Rollback: if ed13 must be reverted, restore `--reason` as required and drop `--class`; callers that migrated to `--class` revert by removing it (field absence is already tolerated by readers).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the `_write_link_event` guard-bypass rationale and the `is_authoritative`/`backfill_classified` segregation contract in a persistent design artifact (ADR, an AGENTS.md section, or a `docs/metrics-architecture.md`) so they survive ticket closure.
evidence: "graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, 'caused_by', ...) **directly**, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards"
"Without this, every auto-derived edge would be silently swallowed and the feature would be a dead letter"
"is_authoritative() returns False for it so it is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series"
No mention of an ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces at least two novel architectural decisions that are not captured in any persistent design document (ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc update): (1) the deliberate bypass of `graph._links._write_link_event` that skips the closed-source and cycle guards — a pattern future maintainers could mistake for a bug and 'fix', silently breaking auto-derived provenance edges; (2) the `is_authoritative()` / `backfill_classified` segregation contract that governs how the metric registry treats backfilled data. Both decisions are explained only in the plan narrative, which is not a durable artifact once the tickets close.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Auto-link vs the closed-source guard (555e ordering)' and 'Every backfilled/classified value is labeled source=backfill_classified … is_authoritative() returns False'
scenarios: A future maintainer refactoring `_links.py` sees `_write_link_event` called directly from the transition command, assumes it is an oversight that bypasses intentional guards, and replaces it with `add_dependency` — silently dropping all future auto-derived `caused_by` edges with no test failure if the guard path is not tested.

A contributor adding a new metric source misreads the `is_authoritative()` contract (not documented outside the plan) and marks a classified backfill as authoritative, corrupting the structural series — there is no design doc to check.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief `docs/metrics-architecture.md` (or an AGENTS.md section) covering: (a) why `_write_link_event` is called directly for `caused_by` auto-derivation and what invariants that relies on; (b) the `source`/`confidence` vocabulary and the `is_authoritative()` contract. Reference this doc from both the `555e` and `9a5a` child ticket descriptions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create an implementation ticket for the historical `close_class` backfill covering: how it writes values (append-only event vs. direct state mutation), idempotency (skip-if-already-set), batching, and the post-rollback schema state (what remains in the store if the backfill is abandoned midway).
evidence: Epic plan Decomposition: 'Backfill (one-time, labeled): caused_by over existing bugs; historical bug --class from structural signals; complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits'
Ticket 3c07 returns 'Ticket not found' — no implementing ticket exists.
ABSENCE: no script file, no AC, no idempotency guarantee, no batch spec for the close_class historical backfill.
findings.finding: The 'historical bug `--class` from structural signals' backfill is named in the Decomposition track but has no implementation ticket (ticket 3c07 returns not-found), and the plan specifies no batching, resumability, dual-write window handling, or rollback path for it.
findings.location: Decomposition § Backfill track: 'historical bug `--class` from structural signals; complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits'
scenarios: The historical close_class backfill writes new STATUS-like events or mutates existing state for hundreds of bugs; if it fails halfway the store is in a partially-backfilled state with no resumption path documented.
A downstream consumer sees a mix of backfilled and non-backfilled bugs during the run window, with no dual-write handling described.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the complete `--class` enum value set in this plan (e.g. `regression | plan_defect | flaky | external | unclassified`) so ticket `ed13` is executable without clarification.
evidence: 'bug classification via a persisted close `--class` enum replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text reason'
'regression vs plan-defect vs flaky vs …' — the trailing ellipsis is an explicit incompleteness marker
'its error message must enumerate the allowed values' — impossible to write without the value list
The Decomposition names `ed13` as a child ticket but its content is not present in this plan
findings.finding: The `--class` enum values for bug-close classification are never enumerated in the plan. The plan uses 'regression vs plan-defect vs flaky vs …' with an explicit ellipsis, deferring the full value set to the executor. An agent implementing ticket `ed13` cannot write the enum definition, validation logic, error message (which the plan requires enumerate allowed values), or tests without knowing the complete value list.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'bug classification via a persisted close `--class` enum'; Approach — 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13)'; Decomposition — Instrumentation track
scenarios: Agent begins implementing `ed13`, reaches the enum definition, has no values to write, must stop and ask
Error message cannot be written; the AC requiring it to enumerate values cannot be satisfied
Tests asserting valid/invalid values cannot be written without the set
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection to the Instrumentation track listing the exact allowed values, e.g.: `regression` (a prior passing test now fails), `plan_defect` (wrong design from the start), `flaky` (non-deterministic), `external` (infra/dependency), `unclassified` (fallback). The error message and enum definition can then reference this list directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default sampling strategy for complexity/clone backfill snapshots (e.g. 'one snapshot per merged PR to the main branch, or weekly if more than 52 PRs exist in the range') so the backfill adapter is implementable without clarification.
evidence: 'complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits' — 'sampled' names a strategy without defining it
No other plan section defines the sampling rate or criterion
findings.finding: The sampling strategy for complexity/clone snapshots ('complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits') is unspecified. 'Sampled' is a placeholder for a real decision — an agent cannot determine whether to sample every Nth commit, every merged PR, every calendar week, or some other interval.
findings.location: Decomposition — Backfill track: 'complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits'
scenarios: Agent implementing the backfill adapter must choose a sampling strategy, leading to arbitrary or inconsistent results
Two agents implementing independently would produce different snapshot densities
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Sample at each merge commit to the default branch, capped at one per calendar week to bound runtime.' This is a defaultable gap — state the default rather than leave it open.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the ERROR gate-sidecar emission: e.g., assert that when a gate run terminates with an error outcome the written sidecar carries `outcome=ERROR` and that the metrics reader can derive a diagnosis interval from it.
evidence: Success Criteria: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
Approach: 'add the smallest possible passive emission only where a signal genuinely leaves no trace, always stamped by trustworthy code (gate error handlers, git blame)'
ABSENCE: no AC reads 'gate sidecar carries ERROR outcome' or 'diagnosis interval is derivable from event store'
findings.finding: Tier-C blindspot (a) — env/integration-diagnosis capture via ERROR gate sidecar — is a named deliverable in Success Criteria and Approach but has no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Success Criteria (Tier-C blindspots bullet) and Approach (gate error handlers); no corresponding AC
scenarios: Implementer delivers the ERROR sidecar path; no test catches a regression or omission because no AC exercises it.
Metrics command reports diagnosis intervals as unavailable even after the emission is wired, because nothing forced the wiring to be verified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'An integration or unit test confirms that a gate-error handler writes a sidecar with `outcome=ERROR` and that the metrics command derives a non-zero diagnosis-interval data point from a seed event store containing such a record.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the live `caused_by` auto-derivation path: e.g., assert that closing a bug ticket where blame can resolve to a single prior commit writes a `caused_by` link event in the store with `confidence=classified` and `source` != `backfill_classified`.
evidence: Approach: '555e writes the edge via graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", ...) directly, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards — Without this, every auto-derived edge would be silently swallowed and the feature would be a dead letter.'

AC 5: 'at least one backfilled caused_by/close_class/snapshot value exists carrying source=backfill_classified + confidence=classified, asserted by a test over the backfill output' — this covers only backfilled edges, not live auto-derived ones.
ABSENCE: no AC asserts that closing a bug with a fixable blame target actually writes a caused_by link to the event store at close-time.
findings.finding: Going-forward `caused_by` auto-derivation (555e live path via `_write_link_event`) is described in detail in the Approach but no AC covers the live path — only the backfill track is tested.
findings.location: Approach section ('Auto-link vs the closed-source guard (555e ordering)'); Decomposition (Instrumentation track, 'blame-based caused_by auto-derivation'); Acceptance Criteria bullet 5 covers backfill only
scenarios: The `_write_link_event` bypass is silently dropped or misconfigured; all ACs still pass because the backfill path is separate and the live path is never exercised by any criterion.
A refactor of `_links.py` removes or changes the private method; the feature becomes a dead letter as the Approach explicitly warns, but no AC catches it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) verifying that `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class <x> --caused-by <id>` writes a `caused_by` link to the event store pointing to `<id>`, confirming the override path is wired.
evidence: Approach: 'When a bug has no file_impact, spans multiple files ambiguously, or blame cannot resolve a single commit, auto-derivation is skipped... the explicit --caused-by <id> override always wins'
ABSENCE: no AC exercises --caused-by or verifies that a manually supplied override is written to the event store.
findings.finding: The `--caused-by <id>` explicit override parameter is described in both Approach and Decomposition but has no covering acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Approach: 'the explicit --caused-by <id> override always wins'; Decomposition: 'blame-based caused_by auto-derivation + --caused-by param + bug-template advisory'
scenarios: The override param is implemented but wired incorrectly (ignored, or treated as informational only); no AC catches it.
The param is never implemented at all; the feature silently degrades to blame-only with no human escape hatch.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RED fixture (failing test written before 555e implementation) that calls `_write_link_event` on a closed-bug source and asserts the edge is persisted, confirming the bypass assumption before the full 555e build-out proceeds.
evidence: 'So 555e writes the edge via `graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", ...)` directly, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards'
'Without this, every auto-derived edge would be silently swallowed and the feature would be a dead letter'
No mention of a RED test or spike to verify `_write_link_event` actually writes the edge for a closed source before 555e full build-out
The plan cites `_links.py:242-247` but does not specify a test that exercises this exact path
findings.finding: The plan asserts — without a RED fixture or probe — that calling `graph._links._write_link_event` directly (bypassing `add_dependency`) correctly writes the `caused_by` edge for a closed bug source, citing `_links.py:242-247` as evidence that the closed-source guard lives only in `add_dependency`. If this assumption is wrong (guard is also in `_write_link_event`, or the method signature/behavior has changed), every auto-derived `caused_by` edge is silently dropped, making the entire Tier-C provenance feature a dead letter with no runtime error.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Auto-link vs the closed-source guard (555e ordering)' and 555e ticket
scenarios: If `_write_link_event` was refactored to also enforce the closed-source guard (or if the author's reading of lines 242-247 is incorrect), the auto-link silently does nothing — no exception, no log, no metric — and bug provenance is never captured
A future refactor of `_links.py` moves the guard into `_write_link_event` without updating 555e, silently breaking provenance capture
findings.suggested_fix: In 555e's pre-implementation checklist, add: 'Write a unit RED test: close a bug, then call `_write_link_event(closed_bug, culprit, "caused_by", ...)` directly and assert the link event appears in the event log. This test failing means the bypass assumption is wrong and the design must be revised before any further implementation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC per Tier-C passive-emission mechanism that directly exercises the triggering flow and asserts the emitted artifact (e.g., a test that runs a gate in ERROR mode and asserts the sidecar carries `outcome=ERROR`; a test that closes a single-file-impact bug and asserts a `caused_by` edge exists on the closed ticket).
evidence: Success Criteria: 'Tier-C blindspots (defined above) are captured passively: env/integration-diagnosis via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars; bug provenance via a new caused_by link relation … bug classification via a persisted close --class enum'
AC bullet 2 tests only that `rebar metrics … --output json` exits 0 and every registered metric key is present or `unavailable` — it does not assert any Tier-C key actually has a value.
AC bullet 4 tests the CLI surface (--class swap) but not that the gate ERROR sidecar is written when a gate errors, nor that blame auto-derivation produces an edge.

ABSENCE: No AC line like 'a test exercises a gate with ERROR outcome and asserts the sidecar contains outcome=ERROR' or 'closing a bug with a single-file impact asserts a caused_by edge exists on the closed ticket'.
findings.finding: The Tier-C passive-emission mechanisms (gate ERROR sidecar write, auto-blame `caused_by` edge on bug close) have no AC-level observable proof that they actually fire in their triggering flows. The AC tests only the `rebar metrics` command output structure and the CLI surface, so a silent failure in the passive instrumentation path (e.g., the gate error handler never writes the sidecar, or blame auto-derivation is always skipped) would leave every registered Tier-C metric in `unavailable` state — and every AC would still pass.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five bullets) vs. Success Criteria bullet 3 ('Tier-C blindspots are captured passively')
scenarios: Gate error handler is implemented but the ERROR sidecar write is accidentally guarded behind a condition that never triggers in the test harness — all Tier-C env-diagnosis metrics remain `unavailable` and no AC fails.

Blame auto-derivation always hits the 'skip' branch (bug has no file_impact in tests) so no caused_by edge is ever written — provenance metrics remain `unavailable` but AC 2 still passes because `unavailable` is a valid response.
findings.suggested_fix: Add three targeted integration/unit tests as ACs: (1) gate harness emits ERROR outcome → assert sidecar file contains `outcome=ERROR` and the gate-economics reader picks it up; (2) `rebar transition <single-file-bug> in_progress closed --class regression` with a seeded blame result → assert `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.links[] | select(.relation=="caused_by")'` exits 0; (3) the `--class` value is persisted → `rebar show <bug> --output json | jq -e '.close_class == "regression"'` (this last one is already partially covered by AC 4 but should be explicit).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Decide explicitly whether `caused_by` should be added to `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` (making it a first-class relation with full API support) or remain a private back-door write, and document the rationale; if back-door, document that `add_dependency('caused_by')` will always raise and what callers must use instead.
evidence: src/rebar/graph/_links.py:19-23: CANONICAL_RELATIONS = frozenset({'blocks','depends_on','relates_to','duplicates','supersedes','discovered_from'})
src/rebar/graph/_links.py:157-159: add_dependency raises ValueError if relation not in CANONICAL_RELATIONS
Plan: '555e writes the edge via graph._links._write_link_event(bug, culprit, "caused_by", ...) directly, bypassing the closed-source and cycle guards'
Plan never mentions adding caused_by to CANONICAL_RELATIONS
src/rebar/graph/__init__.py:21: add_dependency is the public API — it rejects caused_by
findings.finding: The plan adds `caused_by` as a link relation written via `_write_link_event` directly, but never adds it to `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` (`_links.py:19-23`). This creates a permanent split: `add_dependency` raises `ValueError('invalid relation')` for `caused_by`, `_is_active_link` works (no relation validation), and any future caller using the public API to check or add a `caused_by` edge will fail. The plan never discusses whether `caused_by` should be registered in `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` or remain permanently a back-door write — an unaddressed architectural inconsistency with the existing link-relation design.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Auto-link vs the closed-source guard (555e ordering)' / Instrumentation track in Decomposition
scenarios: A future ticket tries to `rebar link <bug> <culprit> caused_by` via the CLI — add_dependency raises ValueError, confusing users
The metrics reader calling `_is_active_link` for caused_by works but calling `add_dependency` for any backfill or repair path fails
fsck or graph traversal logic that iterates CANONICAL_RELATIONS misses caused_by edges entirely
findings.suggested_fix: Add `caused_by` to `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` in `_links.py` (it is non-blocking and non-cycle-inducing like `discovered_from`), then use `add_dependency` with a special-case flag to skip the closed-source guard for this relation, rather than calling `_write_link_event` directly — preserving idempotency and making the relation a proper citizen of the graph API.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit prior-art justification for the single-file NDJSON snapshot store vs. (a) per-snapshot sidecar events in the existing event store, (b) SQLite in `.rebar/`, and address the concurrent-write / merge-conflict risk for a single shared file in the tickets-branch store.
evidence: Plan: '.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson — a git-tracked path in the rebar store — NOT under reports/, which is gitignored (.gitignore:74)'
No comparative analysis vs SQLite in .rebar/, sidecar-event model, or any other approach

The existing REVIEW_RESULT / COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar model already handles going-forward per-ticket event series within the same store substrate — the plan does not explain why per-ticket sidecars are insufficient for metric snapshots
DevLake / Swarmia cited as prior art but neither is present in the codebase or linked documentation
findings.finding: The plan chooses `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` as a git-tracked going-forward snapshot store, but provides no comparative justification vs. established alternatives (SQLite in `.rebar/`, Prometheus push-gateway, or simply tagging each snapshot as a reducer-ignored sidecar event in the existing event store). The only rationale given is avoiding `reports/` (gitignored). A git-tracked NDJSON file that grows with every snapshot incurs write amplification — each going-forward snapshot creates a git commit on the tickets branch — and no prior-art analysis is present for this pattern relative to rebar's existing store substrate or community practice for embedded time-series.
findings.location: Approach section: 'Snapshot store path. The going-forward snapshot series (3275) is committed to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`'
scenarios: NDJSON file grows unboundedly (no pruning strategy specified), creating an ever-larger git object in the tickets branch

Concurrent agents both append to metrics-snapshots.ndjson in the same git commit window, causing a merge conflict on a plain file rather than the per-ticket isolated directory model the store is designed around

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify (or create) the child ticket that owns emitting the ERROR outcome on gate sidecars from gate error handlers, and specify the schema tag and emission point so Tier-C blindspot (a) is concretely covered in the decomposition.
evidence: Plan Success Criteria: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
Existing sidecar schemas in src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py: SCHEMA='completion_verifier_fail_v1', SCHEMA_PASS='completion_verifier_pass_v1' — no ERROR schema tag exists
ABSENCE: no child ticket in visible decomposition (ed13, 555e, 0b96, 9a5a, 18e6, 7931, 3275, c085, 2f8e) names 'gate error handler' or 'ERROR outcome emission' as its deliverable

Partially-verified: the sidecar write path (append_event) exists and supports arbitrary schema tags, so adding ERROR is technically feasible, but the emission point (gate error handlers) is unspecified
findings.finding: The ERROR outcome on existing gate sidecars — the mechanism the plan uses to capture env/integration-diagnosis intervals (Tier-C blindspot a) — is asserted as a reuse of existing sidecars but the existing sidecar schemas (COMPLETION_VERDICT: 'completion_verifier_fail_v1' / 'completion_verifier_pass_v1'; REVIEW_RESULT: plan-review verdicts) contain no ERROR outcome schema. Adding ERROR requires a new schema tag on an existing event type, new emission logic in gate error handlers, and a new reader in the metrics command. No child ticket in the visible decomposition explicitly implements this: the Decomposition says the Command track includes 'gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path' but this child ticket is not visible (ticket c085 covers the registry, 18e6 covers event derivation, 7931 covers git derivation, 3275 covers snapshots, 9a5a covers the CLI — none names the ERROR-path emission).
findings.location: Success Criteria ('env/integration-diagnosis via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars'), Approach section, Decomposition ('gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path')
scenarios: If no child ticket explicitly owns 'emit ERROR outcome from gate error handlers', the Tier-C blindspot (a) capture is never implemented despite being in the Success Criteria, and the gate-sidecar reader in the metrics command has nothing to read.
If a developer implements the ERROR emission without a schema contract, the metrics reader cannot reliably distinguish ERROR sidecars from FAIL sidecars.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `ERROR` outcome vocabulary for gate sidecars: specify which field carries the value (e.g. a new `outcome` field on `COMPLETION_VERDICT` distinct from `verdict`, or an explicit `error_kind` field), enumerate its allowed values, and document the decision rule that distinguishes an infrastructure/env failure from a plan-defect failure — co-located with the sidecar schema or in the missing child ticket `3c07`.
evidence: Plan Success Criteria: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
src/rebar/schemas/completion_verdict.schema.json: verdict field described as 'PASS or FAIL (normalized by the operation)' — no ERROR value defined
src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py:28: EVENT_TYPE = 'COMPLETION_VERDICT'; SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1' — no ERROR schema variant
Ticket 9a5a deps include 3c07-27ac-4b61-47a3 (gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path) but `rebar show 3c07` returns 'Ticket not found'
No file in src/rebar/ contains 'ERROR' as a sidecar outcome value
findings.finding: The `ERROR` outcome value introduced for gate sidecars (Tier-C blindspot-a capture) is referenced in the plan's Success Criteria but has no schema definition: the existing `COMPLETION_VERDICT` schema only defines `PASS`/`FAIL` verdicts, no `ERROR` field or value is defined in any schema file, and child ticket `3c07` ('gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path') — which is the sole described home for this definition — is unreachable from the ticket store.
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars'; Decomposition: 'gate-sidecar reader + `ERROR` path'; 9a5a deps: `3c07-27ac-4b61-47a3`
scenarios: An implementer of the gate-sidecar reader has no schema to validate against and no field name to query for ERROR vs FAIL vs PASS — the feature cannot be implemented as specified.
The metrics reader for env/integration-diagnosis intervals cannot distinguish an infrastructure failure from a plan-defect failure without a discriminating field definition.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `error_kind` field to the `COMPLETION_VERDICT` schema (e.g. `{"error_kind": {"type": "string", "enum": ["env_setup", "infra", "timeout"]}}`) emitted by gate error handlers, and define the diagnosis-interval derivation rule as: interval starts at the first sidecar with `error_kind` set and ends at the next sidecar with `verdict=PASS`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the interval-derivation decision rule with the sidecar schema: specify the exact field (e.g. `outcome` or `error_kind`) and value(s) that mark the start of a diagnosis interval, and what sidecar event (e.g. `verdict=PASS` on the same ticket) closes it.
evidence: Plan Tier-C definition: 'an infra failure starts a diagnosis, its next clean gate ends it' — no definition of 'infra failure' in schema terms
Existing COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecar (completion_sidecar.py) only records PASS/FAIL outcomes with no infrastructure-error discriminator
No child ticket in the accessible set defines the field that distinguishes env/infra failures from plan-defect failures in the sidecar payload
findings.finding: The processing protocol for env/integration-diagnosis interval derivation — 'an infra failure starts a diagnosis, its next clean gate ends it' — is stated as a decision rule in the plan but the discriminating criterion ('infra failure' vs 'plan-defect failure') is never defined at or near any schema, leaving the interval-start condition ambiguous for the sidecar reader implementer.
findings.location: Tier-C blindspots definition: 'env/integration-diagnosis intervals (an infra failure starts a diagnosis, its next clean gate ends it)'; Decomposition: 'gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path'
scenarios: The sidecar reader (3c07) must decide which sidecar records open a diagnosis interval — without a field definition, it cannot discriminate between a legitimate FAIL (plan defect) and an infra FAIL (env failure).
The resulting metric silently conflates plan-defect retry loops with infra-diagnosis intervals, producing misleading trend data.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the full `--class` enum vocabulary (regression, plan_defect, env_integration, flaky, preexisting, not_a_bug, duplicate, escalated, undetermined) from child ticket `ed13` into the epic plan's Success Criteria or Approach section so an executor reading only the parent plan has the complete bounded vocabulary.
evidence: Parent plan Tier-C definition uses 'regression vs plan-defect vs flaky vs …' — explicit ellipsis, incomplete vocabulary
Parent plan Approach section says 'its error message must enumerate the allowed values' but does not list them
Child ticket ed13 defines the full 9-value set: regression, plan_defect, env_integration, flaky, preexisting, not_a_bug, duplicate, escalated, undetermined
src/rebar/_commands/txn.py and transition_close.py do not yet contain close_class (confirmed by search returning no matches in src/)
findings.finding: The `--class` enum value vocabulary is defined only in child ticket `ed13` and not in the parent plan, which uses the phrase 'regression vs plan-defect vs flaky vs …' with an explicit ellipsis. An executor implementing from the epic plan alone cannot know the full bounded enum required to write the validator, the error message listing allowed values, or the schema.
findings.location: Tier-C blindspots: 'bug outcome classification (regression vs plan-defect vs flaky vs …)'; Approach: 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13): its error message must enumerate the allowed values'
scenarios: An implementer reading only the epic plan to implement `ed13` cannot write the validator or the error message, since the full enum is not in the plan.
The epic-level AC ('bounded --class enum persisted on the STATUS event') cannot be verified without knowing what 'bounded' means.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Success Criteria, replace 'bug classification via a persisted close `--class` enum' with 'bug classification via a persisted close `--class` enum (`regression | plan_defect | env_integration | flaky | preexisting | not_a_bug | duplicate | escalated | undetermined`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly declare 555e as a prerequisite of 2f8e in 2f8e's description/ACs, and add the 555e→2f8e ordering to the parent's cross-ticket coordination note.
evidence: 2f8e Approach: 'Reuse the same conservative single-culprit resolver as the live auto-derivation … reuse src/rebar/metrics/blame.py'
555e Files: 'src/rebar/metrics/blame.py — new: single-culprit resolver'
Parent cross-ticket coordination note enumerates ed13→555e and 0b96→9a5a ordering but does not mention 555e→2f8e
findings.finding: Child 2f8e has an undeclared hard dependency on child 555e: 2f8e's approach states it 'reuse[s] src/rebar/metrics/blame.py' which is created by 555e, but 2f8e's ACs and the sibling roster ordering notes do not declare 555e as a prerequisite. If 2f8e is executed before 555e, the import fails and the backfill script is broken.
findings.location: child 2f8e-0f7e-6fea-4825 — Approach section ('reuse src/rebar/metrics/blame.py')
scenarios: A developer picks up 2f8e to run the backfill before 555e is merged; the import of blame.py fails with ModuleNotFoundError; the backfill script cannot run at all
CI runs 2f8e tests without 555e in the environment and the test suite fails with an import error rather than a meaningful test failure
findings.suggested_fix: In 2f8e's What/Approach section add: 'Depends on 555e (blame.py must be landed first).' In the parent Decomposition cross-ticket coordination block add: '2f8e depends on 555e (blame.py); land 555e before 2f8e.'

findings.finding: The caused_by auto-link fires after transition_core commits the STATUS event (to access the fixing commit for blame), but add_dependency in graph/_links.py:242-247 unconditionally rejects writes where the source ticket is already closed — so every auto-derived caused_by link will silently fail (best-effort swallows the ValueError), leaving the feature a no-op for auto-derivation.
findings.location: Approach section ('blame-based auto-derivation'), ticket 555e (transition_close.py auto-link), src/rebar/graph/_links.py lines 242-247
findings.suggested_fix: Exempt non-blocking relations (relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from, and the new caused_by) from the closed-source guard in add_dependency, since these relations carry no scheduling semantics and a closed ticket legitimately receives them as observability links. Or, have 555e's auto-link call _write_link_event directly (the lower-level writer that bypasses the guard), documenting why the cycle/closed checks are not needed for a non-blocking, non-cycle-inducing relation on a closed source.

findings.finding: The plan (ticket 3275) asserts that `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` will be a 'committed artifact' mirroring 'the existing `reports/*.report.json` precedent', but `.gitignore` line 74 shows `/reports/` is gitignored at the repo root. The only `reports/` files referenced in docs (e.g. `d01e-live-matrix.report.json` in ADR-0037) are CI output artifacts that are never committed. If `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` lives under `/reports/`, git will ignore it, no snapshots will ever accumulate in the repo, and the going-forward trend capability will silently not work.
findings.location: Decomposition / Backfill track; ticket 3275 (Going-forward metrics snapshot writer/reader), Files section: '`reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — new committed artifact (one snapshot per line), mirroring the existing `reports/*.report.json` precedent'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the snapshot store to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (already tracked as part of the rebar store) or `data/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`, and update ticket 3275's file_impact and the Backfill track accordingly. Alternatively, add `!reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` to `.gitignore` if keeping the `reports/` location, with an explicit CI step that commits the file.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
